transcript.content:
You are a news summarization assistant. Summarize the following article objectively and extract its key facts. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object вЂ” no prose, no markdown fences:
{
  "summary": "string (150-200 words, neutral tone)",
  "key_facts": ["string","string","string","string","string"],
  "sources_represented": ["string"]
}
Rules:
- summary must be 150-200 words
- key_facts must contain EXACTLY 5 items вЂ” the 5 most objectively significant facts
- sources_represented must list every distinct perspective or source type included
- Do not editorialize, adopt the article's framing, or use loaded language
- Represent all perspectives present in the article proportionally

Summarize this article:

---
HEADLINE: Classes of more than 70 students amid historic declines in test scores 02:12 ГўВЂВў Source: CNN Classes o
SOURCE: CNN (Opinion)

ARTICLE:

Classes of more than 70 students amid historic declines in test scores 02:12 ГўВЂВў Source: CNN Classes of more than 70 students amid historic declines in test scores 02:12 CNN вЂ” Students who struggled through the height of the Covid-19 pandemic ГўВЂВ“ between March 2020 and early 2023 ГўВЂВ“ are still having trouble keeping up with course work, according to a study from the nonprofit NWEA, which focuses on education research. ГўВЂВњThis analysis provides the most current evidence to help guide recovery efforts and resource allocations in support of schools,ГўВЂВќ an NWEA news release said. ГўВЂВњWhile the pandemic is now deemed over, the impacts on students based on two markers, achievement in reading and mathematics, are still apparent.ГўВЂВќ Sixth-grade students in Topeka, Kansas, set up their lockers on the first day of classes in August 2022. Evert Nelson/The Capital-Journal/USA Today Network Related article Test scores for 13-year-olds drop several points since the start of pandemic, building on decade-long decline, report says The study used data from 6.7 million public school students from grades 3-8, the release said. According to the study, ГўВЂВњIn nearly all grades, achievement gains during 2022-23 fell short of prepandemic trends, which stalled progress toward pandemic recovery.ГўВЂВќ The performance gap widened in some grades when comparing students affected by Covid-19 and those before the pandemic, the data showed. The study found that students need an additional 4.1 months to catch up in reading and 4.5 months of additional instruction to catch up in math. Among those students, Black and Hispanic students ГўВЂВњremain furthest from recovery,ГўВЂВќ the release said. ГўВЂВњCOVID-19 may no longer be an emergency, but we are very much still dealing with the fallout from the crisis,ГўВЂВќ said Dr. Karyn Lewis, co-author of the study and director of the Center for School and Student Progress at NWEA, according to the release. Some students in the US are struggling with math and reading after the Covid-19 pandemic, a new study shows. Jose Luis Pelaez Inc/Digital Vision/Getty Images ГўВЂВњThese data reiterate that recovery will not be linear, easy, or quick and we cannot take our foot off the gas pedal,ГўВЂВќ Lewis said. ГўВЂВњDisappointing as these results may be, itГўВЂВ™s important not to lose sight of the fact that things would likely be so much worse without the enduring work of educators and schools to support students in this moment.ГўВЂВќ The NWEAГўВЂВ™s recommendations include using local data to help students continue to recover, intervening as needed and communicating important information with families in a timely way. The NWEA describes itself as a ГўВЂВњpioneer in educational research and assessment methodology,ГўВЂВќ with over four decades of work.
---

{
  "summary": "A recent study by the nonprofit NWEA, which analyzed data from 6.7 million public school students in grades 3-8, reveals that students who experienced the peak of the Covid-19 pandemic are still struggling to meet academic expectations. The study highlights a persistent decline in reading and mathematics achievement, with progress during the 2022-23 school year lagging behind pre-pandemic trends, thereby delaying overall recovery. Students are estimated to require an additional 4.1 months to catch up in reading and 4.5 months in math. Notably, performance gaps have widened, particularly affecting Black and Hispanic students who remain the furthest from recovery. The NWEA urges continued support for educational recovery efforts, emphasizing that the impacts of the pandemic on student achievement remain significant despite the official end of the emergency. Recommendations include utilizing local data for targeted interventions and maintaining effective communication with families to support ongoing student progress.",
  "key_facts": [
    "The NWEA study analyzed data from 6.7 million students in grades 3-8 across the U.S.",
    "Achievement gains in reading and mathematics during 2022-23 were below pre-pandemic trends.",
    "Students need an estimated additional 4.1 months for reading and 4.5 months for math to catch up.",
    "The post-pandemic performance gap widened, with Black and Hispanic students most affected.",
    "The NWEA recommends using local data, interventions, and family communication to aid recovery."
  ],
  "sources_represented": [
    "NWEA (educational research nonprofit)",
    "CNN (news reporting)",
    "Dr. Karyn Lewis (NWEA researcher and study co-author)"
  ]
}

parsed_output.summary: A recent study by the nonprofit NWEA, which analyzed data from 6.7 million public school students in grades 3-8, reveals that students who experienced the peak of the Covid-19 pandemic are still struggling to meet academic expectations. The study highlights a persistent decline in reading and mathematics achievement, with progress during the 2022-23 school year lagging behind pre-pandemic trends, thereby delaying overall recovery. Students are estimated to require an additional 4.1 months to catch up in reading and 4.5 months in math. Notably, performance gaps have widened, particularly affecting Black and Hispanic students who remain the furthest from recovery. The NWEA urges continued support for educational recovery efforts, emphasizing that the impacts of the pandemic on student achievement remain significant despite the official end of the emergency. Recommendations include utilizing local data for targeted interventions and maintaining effective communication with families to support ongoing student progress.
key_facts: The NWEA study analyzed data from 6.7 million students in grades 3-8 across the U.S.
Achievement gains in reading and mathematics during 2022-23 were below pre-pandemic trends.
Students need an estimated additional 4.1 months for reading and 4.5 months for math to catch up.
The post-pandemic performance gap widened, with Black and Hispanic students most affected.
The NWEA recommends using local data, interventions, and family communication to aid recovery.
sources_represented: Dr. Karyn Lewis (NWEA researcher and study co-author)
